figures.caption: Figure 17: Representative ground-truth Burgers’ fields generated using numerical solver. The plots display the fields for two selected systems (System 1 and 14) from the dataset of K = 20 systems. The red markers indicate spatial locations of observation data.

Figure 5: Comparison of closure model estimation and surrogate performance across different models with K = 20 in Experiment 1. The rows correspond to Numerical Solver, Supervised FNO, PhysicsBased FNO, and PINNs, respectively. Left Column: Comparison between the learned closure fα(u̇) (orange) and the ground truth f(u̇) (blue) over the velocity range u̇ ∈ [−6, 6]. Middle & Right Columns: Surrogate predictions of displacement u(t) for two selected systems (System 3 and System 9). Bottom Panel: Histogram of velocity values in GT dataset.

Figure 6: Trace plots illustrating the evolution of a randomly selected MALA chain for stiffness k of System 9 across four models with K = 20 in Experiment 1. The figures illustrate convergence behaviour relative to the GT parameter value (green dashed line).
Table 7: Network architectures for surrogate and closure models in Experiment 1.
Table 9: Network architectures for surrogate and closure models in Experiment 2.
Table 12: Hyperparameter settings for optimization and training in Experiment 3.

Figure 13: Comparison of closure model estimation and surrogate performance across different models with K = 20 in Experiment 2. The rows correspond to GT field generated by fixed-point solver, and results from Supervised FNO, Physics-Based FNO, and PINNs, respectively. Left Column: Comparison between the learned closure model fα(u) (orange) and the ground truth f(u) (blue). Middle & Right Columns: Surrogate predictions for two systems (System 8 and System 19). Bottom Panel: Histogram of u in GT dataset.

Figure 2: Overview of the proposed framework. The algorithm alternates between three main stages: (1) Hierarchical posterior sampling using ensemble MALA to update parameter chains, (2) Lower-level surrogate training optimizing the surrogate model F β parameterized by β over N iterations, where the loss function LSurrogate could take the forms in (27), (28) and (29), and (3) Upper-level closure learning optimizing the closure model fα parameterized by α to discover the unknown nonlinear dynamics, where the loss function LLML follows (26).
Table 2: Comparison of hierarchical and non-hierarchical frameworks trained using PINNs as surrogate model in Experiment 1. Reported metrics are the same as in Table 1.

Figure 8: Comparison of posterior parameter distributions between Hierarchical and NonHierarchical Bayesian frameworks using a PINN-based surrogate with K = 20 in Experiment 1. The figure compares the uncertainty quantification performance for three parameters from System 3. The top row (Hierarchical) and bottom row (Non-Hierarchical) show distinct posterior densities. GT values are indicated by green dashed lines, overlaid on the posterior samples (blue histograms) and their PDF approximations (red curves).

Figure 14: Posterior histograms and corresponding approximated posterior density functions for three parameters from System 9 across three models with K = 20 in Experiment 2. Each panel shows the marginal posterior distribution obtained from the final 1,000 samples of one random MALA chain after convergence.

Figure 4: Displacement trajectories u(t) for 20 nonlinear mass–damper systems. These systems are governed by (31) and solved using the leapfrog integrator with system-specific parameters in Figure 3. The right two panels illustrate, for two representative systems, the corresponding sparse and noisy observations generated via observation operator g(k).

Figure 3: Distribution of ground-truth physical parameters for K = 20 mass–damper systems in Experiment 1. Each system’s parameter vector θ = {log(k), u0, v0} is sampled from the hierarchical prior with hyperparameters µϕ = {log(5.0), 0.0, 2.0} and τϕ = {0.03, 2.0, 2.0}. The histograms show the parameter distributions across the 20 systems, while the overlaid curves represent the corresponding ground-truth probability density functions implied by the hierarchical prior. The green dashed lines indicate ground-truth population means. The apparent deviation of the histograms from the ground-truth curves occurs because a limited population of only 20 systems poorly represents the full underlying probability distribution.

Table 3: Computational efficiency comparison across considered models for varying number of systems (K ∈ {5,10,20,30,50,100}) in Experiment 1. The reported values represent the average time (in seconds) required to complete one full training epoch within the bilevel framework.

Figure 9: MALA chains of posterior samples for stiffness k of System 9 for Hierarchical and NonHierarchical Bayesian frameworks in Experiment 1. The figures compare the convergence rate between hierarchical and non-hierarchical models.

Figure 10: MALA chains of posterior samples for hyperparameters inferred using the PINN-based hierarchical Bayesian framework in Experiment 1. The panels display the MALA chains for µϕ and τϕ. The top row corresponds hyperprior means (µk, µu0 , µv0), while the bottom row shows the variances (τk, τu0 , τv0). Green dashed lines represent ground-truth values.

Figure 7: Posterior histograms and corresponding approximated probability density functions for three parameters from System 9 across four models with K = 20 in Experiment 1. Each panel shows the marginal posterior distribution obtained from the final 1,000 samples of one random MALA chain after convergence. The blue bars represent the posterior samples, the red curve denotes the approximated density, and the green dashed line marks the GT parameter value.

Figure 19: MALA traces and posterior histograms of parameters from System 14 in Experiment 3. Top row: MALA traces of parameters ν and z. Bottom row: posterior histograms of last 1,000 samples of MALA traces and their corresponding PDF approximations.

Figure 18: Closure model estimation and surrogate performance of supervised FNO with K = 20 in Experiment 3. Left: Closure model estimation with histograms of ground-truth velocity data provided. Middle & Right: Ground truth and surrogate prediction of solution field of System 14.

Table 4: Quantitative comparison of three models across varying numbers of systems (K ∈ {10,20,30}) in Experiment 2. The table evaluates Parameter Inference, Closure MSE and Surrogate MSE with metrics the same as in Table 1. Closure MSE is evaluated over range u ∈ [0, 2.5].

Figure 15: Comparison of posterior parameter distributions between Hierarchical and NonHierarchical Bayesian frameworks using a PINN-based surrogate with K = 20 in Experiment 2. The figure compares the uncertainty quantification performance for three parameters from System 3 for both hierarchical and non-hierarchical setups.

Figure 12: Representative ground truth Darcy flow fields generated using fixed-point numerical solver. The plots display the fields for three selected systems (System 8, 9, and 19) from the dataset of K = 20 systems. The red markers indicate spatial locations of observation data.

Figure 11: Distribution of ground-truth physical parameters for 20 Darcy flow systems in Experiment 2. Each system’s parameter vector θ = {z1, z2, z3} is sampled from the hierarchical prior with hyperparameters µϕ = {0.2,−0.1, 0.2} and τϕ = {0.5, 0.5, 0.5}.

Table 1: Quantitative comparison of the four models across varying numbers of systems (K ∈ {5,10,20,30,50,100}) in Experiment 1. The table evaluates Parameter Inference performance via the MSE of posterior means relative to GT parameter values and Coverage, defined as the percentage of GT parameters falling within two posterior standard deviations. Additionally, Closure MSE measures the MSE of the learned nonlinear closure fα(u̇) evaluated on u̇ ∈ [−5, 5], while Surrogate MSE quantifies the accuracy of the surrogate model’s trajectory predictions compared to GT trajectories.
